extra.reasoning:
The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false. Transition t5 ("on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 6): sent := true") explicitly sets sent=true while opted_out is true, which is the source of the violation.

Reachability of the violating state:
- Start: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0.
- Event optout (t1, guard true): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0.
- Six tick events (t4, guard true): ticks increments 1,2,3,4,5,6 (caps at 6); each also sets sent=false. Now opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=6.
- Event send_attempt: with opted_out=true and ticks>=6, t5 is enabled and sets sent:=true. (Both t3 and t5 are enabled on this event; t5 is the deliberately-added special-case override for ticks>=6 — it is listed last and is meaningless unless it takes effect, so the intended semantics fire t5.) Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true.

This state has opted_out true and sent true, falsifying globally(opted_out implies not sent). The execution is 8 events, within the 10-event bound. t5 is the design flaw: it permits sending to an opted-out subject once enough ticks have elapsed, contradicting the stated safety intent.
